California
Fair POlitical
Practices Cornmission
May 21, 1990
LyIe L. Lopus
Assistant city Attorney
City Attorney's office
39700 Civic Center Drive
P. o. Box 5006
Fremont, CA 94537
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-9 O- l-5 2
Dear Mr. Lopus:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regard-
ing the duties and responsibi l- ities of a city council-rnember under
the conf l ict-of-interest 
provisions of the PoIitical Reform Act
(the itAggttl.l Your Lettei d.oes not request advice pertaining to a
specific situation before the councilmember but rather seeks
generaL guidelines. I,[oreover, you have not provided us with the
name of the councilmernber whose duties are in question, nor is it
stated in your letter that this person has authorized your request
for advice. Accordingly, \./e are treating your request as one for
informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy
enclosed. ) 2
SpecificaIly, you are requesting advice regarding application
of the nexus provisions of Regutation La7O2.1 (copy enclosed) to a
city councilmember who is a professional real property appraiser.
You.do not presently have actual facts on which to request formal
advice. Hovrever, you expect that circumstances sirniljr to those
posed in your request for advice wilf arise in the near future and
you wish to be prepared to address the issues prornptly. To that
1- covernment code sections 81ooo-91o15. Alr statutory references
are to the covernment code unless otherwise indicated. 
commission
regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title 
?
Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
)- Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
j-nmunity provided by an opinion -or formal writ€en advice. 
(Sec_
tion 83114; Regulation 19329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8OZ o Sacramcntg CA 95grr.+-116r.\7 o (916) 122-5660


FiLe No. r-9 0- l-52
Page 2
extent, you have posed the following questions for our
consideration.
I{e do not provide advice concerning provisions of law other
than the Act. 
You nay wish to consult wiln tne Attorney ceneral,s
office regarding other provisions of Ia$, such as section 1O9O
which prohibits governnent officials 
from having an interest in
contracts with their agencies.
OUESTIONS
1. A councilmernber is a self-enployed appraiser. 
The
councilmember has performed an appraisal for the overall project
designer for a developer. Upon completion of the plans for the
project, the developer appears before the city councit with a
conplete project proposal which is based in part on the appraisal
performed by the councilmernber. Do the rrnexusrr provisions of the
Act disqualify the councilmember from participating in the deci-
sion to approve or d.i.sapprove the proj ect?
2. A counciLmember is a self-empl-oyed appraiser. prior to
his election to the city council, the city retained his services
to appraise a site considered for acquisition for a park. 
The
city counciL is now considering purchase of the land. Do the
rrnexusrr provisions of the Act disqualify the councilmember fron
participating in decisions regarding purchase of the land?
3. A counciLnember is an enpJ,oyee of a family-operated ap-
praisal, firm. 
The councilme:nber receives a regular salary or in
the alternative receives an hourly rate for each job perforned or
receives a percentage of the f irrnrs charge for each appraisal he
performs. Clients of the firrn appear before the city council or
are indirectly involved in a decision by the city council. 
Do the
rtnexustr provisions of the Act disquality ttre couircilmernber fron
participating in these decisions?
4. For purposes of the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of
the Act, what constitutes a source of incorne for an appraiser
wtrose earnings derive from hourly rrages or a percentagl of the
fees charged by his employing firn for the work he peiforms?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Absent a connection between the appraised value of the
project and the government decision to be trade, g5g r,r.r"*s=ri provi-
sions of the Act do not disgualify a councilmenUer tron --
participating in a decision to approve or disapprove a deveJ.opment
project for which he or she has perfonnea an aiiraisai. 
piease
note, however, that the councilnenber witf neviitheless be
disquarified fron participating in the decision rt itre aeveroper
was a source of income to the councirmember of $250 or nore duri.ng
the 12 months preceding the decision.


File No. I-9 o- 152
Page 3
2. Ttre trnexus provisions of the Act disqualify a
councilmember who appraised the land for the city frorn participat-
ing in a decision to purchase the land for a park. However, the
I'public generallyrr exception applies and the councilmember need
not disqualify frorn participating in the decision.
3. If the councilmernber has an investment of $l-,ooo or
greater in the firm and has a 1o-percent or greater ownership
interest in the firn, then the discussion in ans\^rer to your first
question is equally applicable to these facts. 
A1so, if there is
a nexus between the decision and work previously performed by the
councilmenber in his or her capacity as an appraiser, the
council.member must disqualify.
4. For purposes of the conf lict-of- interest provisions of
the Act, only the firrn is a source of incorne for an appraiser
whose earnings derive from hourly wages or a percentage of the
fees charged by his or her enploying. firrn for the work he or she
performs.
ANALYSIS
Initially 
we point out that you have correctly stated in your
request for advice that the "nexusrr provisions of the Act (Regula-
tion 18702.1(d)) apply when a source of incorne to a public of-
ficial 
is either directly or indirectly involved in a decision
before the officiaL. 
This is so because a public official 
may not
use his or her official 
position to accomplish that for which he
or she receives payrnent in his or her private capacity. 
(chin
Advice Letter, No. A-88-091, copy enclosed.)
The Political 
Reform Act was enacted by the people of the
state of California by initiative 
in 1974. The purpose for the
disclosure and disqualif ication provisions of the Act is to ensure
that public officials, 
rrhether elected or appointed, perform their
duties in an inpartial manner, free from bias caused by their own
financial interests or the financial interests of persons vrho have
supported then. (section 81001(b).) In furtherance of these
objectives, section 87100 prohibits a public official 
fron making,
participating in or attenpting to influence any governnental deci-
sion in which he or she knows or has reason to know he or she has
a financial interest. 
A councilrnember is a public official 
(Sec-
tion 82048. )
An official 
has a financial interest in a governrnental deci-
sion if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision vri11 have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally, on the official 
or a menber of his irnmediate
family or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars (91,000) or more.


File No. 
■-90-■52
Page 4
***
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts.
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution 
in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty 
dol-
lars ($250) or more in value provided to, received
by or promised to the public official within 12
months prior to the tirne when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of nanagement.
***
For purposes of this section, j-ndirect invest-
ment or interest means any investment or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public
official, 
by an agent on behalf of a public of-
ficial, 
or by a business entity or trust in which
the official, 
the official's 
agents, spouse, and
dependent children own directly, indirectly, 
or
beneficially a lo-percent interest or greater.
Section 87103 (a) , (c) , and (d) .
ouestion 1
In your first question, you have asked us to assume that the
councilmember is self enployed. For purposes of our discussion we
also assume that the councihnember has a business position and an
investnent interest in his firn which is greater than $1,000.
(Section 82034.) we also assume that the counc i lmernber ' s firrn is
a source of $250 or nore in incone to the councilmember. Finally,
clients of his firn are sources of income to the councilmember
when the councilnember, s pro rata share of a clientrs pa)rments to
his firro total $250 or more in the twelve-nonth period preceding
the decision. 
(Section 82030(a).) Under this set of facts, the
councilmember is required to disqualify hinself frorn participatJ.ng
in any city decision which rrould foreseeably and materially affect
either his firm or a client lrho is a source of incorne of g25O or
more to the councilmember in the preceding twelve months in a man-
ner that is distinguishabte fron the effect on the public gener-
a11y.
In order to deternine whether the councilmember nust
disqualify hinself, first you must deternine the foreseeabiLity of
a financial effect of the decision upon the counciLnenber,s buli-
ness or any of the clients of the business who have been a source
of income of $250 or nore to the councilnember during the preced-
ing twelve months. If it is foreseeable that the de;ision wiII
have a financial effect, it nust be ascertained whether the


File No. I-9 O- 152
Page 5
financial effect will be rrmaterialrr to determine rrhether the
councilrnenber may participate in the decision. Regulations
lA702.1 and 18702.2 (copy enclosed) set forth the cri.teria that
apply in naking this deternination.
In addition, the councitmember erill be required to disqualify
hinself if there is a I'nexusrr between the purpose for which the
councilmernber received income and the governmental decision. 
A
nexus situati.on exists if the official 
receives incorne to achieve
a goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or
hindered by the decision. (Regulation rA7O2.1(d).) Let us as-
sume, for purposes of our analysis, that an architect-
councilmember prepares a design for the overall project designer
for a developer. when the developer appears before the
councilnember seeking approval for his or her project, the
councilmember will be in a position to approve the design which he
was paid to prepare. Thus, there is a disqualifying nexus because
the public official 
would be using his officiat 
position to ac-
complish that for which he received incorne to accornplish in his
private capacity. Under such circumstances, the councilmember
could not participate in the decision to approve the developer,s
project proposal.
You have advised ne that a developer usuaLly ernploys the
services of an appraiser to deternine the value of land he or she
intends to develop. Based on the appraisal, a developer may
negotiate an option to purchase the land. When the developer ap-
pears before the councit with his or her development proposaL,
approval or disapproval- of the proposed development is unrelated
to the value of the 1and. Any development fees inposed on the
developer are based on population density and not on the value of
the Iand. Therefore, there is no nexus bety/een the appraisal
performed by the councilmember and approvat or disapproval of the
project. 
Hot ever, if the councihnember has received income in
excess of $250 fron the developer in the twelve months preceding
the decision, the councilmember may have to disqualify from
participating in the decision whenever the developer ippears
directly before the city counciL. (Regulation 18702.1.j
Applying our analysis to your facts, vre conclude that if the
appraisal for the overall project designer for a developer
inctuded reconrnendations for further appraisals before lroceeding
with the project there would be a nexul between the purlose for
which the councilmenber received incone and the decilio; before
the council. 
There hrould also be a nexus if approval of the
ploject hinged on the value assigned to the property assessed,
Otherwise, in the absence of a nexus, the only disqualif ication
would be based on the rnateriarity of the effeLt of the decision on
the councilmemberrs sources of income, as discussed above.
Ouestion 2
We proceed now to analyze your second question in vhich you
ask us to assume that the councirmember appiaised a parcer of land


FiIe No. I -9 0- 152
Page 6
under contract with the city prior to his election to the city
council. 
The city is now considering the purchase of the land for
use as a park. In this instance, ttre rrnexusrr provisions of
Regulation La7o2.1(d) apply because the value of the land is
determined by the appraisal and the councilmember would be approv-
ing the purchase based on the appraisal for thich he received pay-
ment. Assuming, however, that no other financial interests
require the councilmenber to abstain frorn participating in the
decision, no disqual ification is required because the decision to
purchase the land for a park will affect the councilnember in a
manner that is not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generall,y. (Regulation 18703, copy enclosed.)
Ouestion 3
In your third question, you ask us to assume that the
councilmember is an employee of a fanily-operated appraisal f irrn.
If the councilrnember has an investment of S1,OOO or greater in the
firm and has a lo-percent or greater ' ownership interest in the
firm, then the discussion in answer to your first question is
equally applicable to these facts. 
(See Sections 82030 and
87103.) Furthernore, if the finn has been a source of income of
$25o or more to the counciLmember during the preceding 12 months,
the councilmember must disqualify hinself or herself from
participating in decisions concerning his or her firrn which will
have a material financial effect upon the finn. 
AIso, if there is
a nexus between the decision and work previously performed by the
councilmember in his or her capacity as an appraiser, the
councilmember must disgualify.
ouestion 4
In response to your fourth question, we conclude that only
the firm is a source of incone to the councilmember. Conversely,
if the councilmember had an investment interest in the finn
greater than $1,0OO, and if the councilmenber owned a lo-percent
interest in the finn or greater, then clients of the firm- erould be
sources of incorne to the councilnember. The amount of incone at-
tributed to the councilrnember in these circunstances would be a
pro rata share of the incoroe received by the firur frorn each cli-
ent. 
(Section 82030. )
Clients of the firm are those individuals rrho do business
with the f irro. Thus, for exanple, if an engineer does lusiness
with the firm on beharf of a devei.oper, the engineer is a client
of the firn and not the developer. -This is so because the
engineer independently initiatls 
the financiar reriiiotr -"itr, tn"
f irrn, provides direction where necessary, and is i"rlo"=iui" 
r".
pa)rment. Thus, the engineer is the sol,E source of iircome to the
f inn and not the developer. (See Advice letters to Hart, No.
A-83-264; Schechtrnan, No. A-87-031i and Huouet, No. IIET_3ao,
copies enclosed. )


FiIe No. I-90-152
' Page 7
I trust this letter adequately responds to your concerns.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.
sincerely,
Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel
%″
By: Blanca l,[. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division
KED:BMB:plh
